feedback.text: I am very excused, but I am really talking about which consumer rights are being violated or reduced in this era of digital goods. Rather, I wanted to talk about the fact that games and other products are being destroyed by their distributors. Especially in recent years, with more and more products being digital, consumers lose access to products they have bought by downloading alone. Picture if you bought a car suddenly lost this car because you no longer support it. What we have asked about “Stop Killing Game” is simple: first, we have asked for single-player games in the future, are always capable of being played on even without the publisher’s support, as it is possible and permitted to make backups of all digital products for external storage if storage of the device fails and downloads the servers then consume it is to restore the output you bought, third, that games with an online component, mainly single player online, have ‘end of Life’/‘end of life’ and, finally, that games in cases where they are sold in phisical shops and have a server dependent online component have a warning, packaging that you say you no longer work or can be operated in the future and that they are dependent on services. For Game-Key for Nintendo Switch 2 and optica media (CD, DVD, Blu-Ray, HVD, GD-Rom, PDD, etc.) containing Launcher (eg: Call of Duty: Black Ops 4, Halo Infinite, Hogwarts Legacy, Battlefield 2042, etc.). Game-Key for Nintendo Switch 2 is a new type of physical cartridge introduced by Nintendo, designed to deal with the growing size of modern game files while keeping production costs controlled. Different from traditional cartridges storing all game data, a Game-Key contains only a licence key that allows users to download the complete game from Nintendo eShop to Console’s internal memory or to a compatible Express microSD card. Thus, the Game-Key system balances convenience and cost, but underlines the need for robust safeguards for digital property in the evolving video game landscape. For example, during the search to write this text I discovered that two disk products/games (Blu-Ray in these cases) contain only Launcher/install. So we call for legislation to be put in place to publicise and maintain servers are necessary to run the digital good (games and other types of softhware) or to return the money to the value that was purchased if the service needed to download or authenticate the product is terminated, if it is physical copia containing only Launcher/installer. It would also be important and essential that digital goods/services onlines/digital services are possible to be inherited from parents for films. It would be important and essential that any product or service I obtained should be able to transfer to my family members when they die or receive a digital product when a relative dies. It would be important for my library in Steam or GOG or Microsoft Store to be inherited by my family members when I died or had already been interested. Or it would be possible for my Google account (gmail, google one) to be inherited from my relatives or descendants and then transferred. An image that had a collection of old watches I bought throughout my life and when I died rather than inherit my descendants, who sold the clock to me collected the watches without economically compensating the descendants. The growing dominance of digital goods and services, as evidenced by the interruption of games such as The Crew and P.T. due to server shutdowns or catalogues removal, highlights a very obliged and excused problem: Tiago Pereira dos Santos Silva hell.cat.forever@gmail.com
